cell_line.prompt_description: Cell line is a group of cells that are genetically identical and have been cultured in a laboratory setting. e.g., HeLa, Jurkat, HEK293, etc. When multiple ontology candidates match, prefer the MOST SPECIFIC descendant term over broader parent terms (e.g., 'CD14-positive monocyte' rather than 'monocyte'; 'cutaneous melanoma' rather than 'melanoma'; 'aortic endothelial cell' rather than 'endothelial cell'). Generalize only when no specific term fits the evidence.

cell_type.prompt_description: Cell type is a classification of cells based on their structure and function, representing normal cells. e.g., neuron, hepatocyte, etc. When multiple ontology candidates match, prefer the MOST SPECIFIC descendant term over broader parent terms (e.g., 'CD14-positive monocyte' rather than 'monocyte'; 'cutaneous melanoma' rather than 'melanoma'; 'aortic endothelial cell' rather than 'endothelial cell'). Generalize only when no specific term fits the evidence.

tissue.prompt_description: Tissue is a group of cells that work together to perform a specific function in an organism. e.g., lung, liver, heart, etc. When multiple ontology candidates match, prefer the MOST SPECIFIC descendant term over broader parent terms (e.g., 'CD14-positive monocyte' rather than 'monocyte'; 'cutaneous melanoma' rather than 'melanoma'; 'aortic endothelial cell' rather than 'endothelial cell'). Generalize only when no specific term fits the evidence.

disease.prompt_description: Disease is an abnormal state of the body or its parts that impairs normal function. e.g., lung cancer, down syndrome, etc. When multiple ontology candidates match, prefer the MOST SPECIFIC descendant term over broader parent terms (e.g., 'CD14-positive monocyte' rather than 'monocyte'; 'cutaneous melanoma' rather than 'melanoma'; 'aortic endothelial cell' rather than 'endothelial cell'). Generalize only when no specific term fits the evidence.

drug.prompt_description: Drug is a chemical or biological substance used to prevent, diagnose, treat, or alleviate disease or symptoms, or to modify physiological functions. e.g., aspirin, metformin, trastuzumab, etc. When multiple ontology candidates match, prefer the MOST SPECIFIC descendant term over broader parent terms (e.g., 'CD14-positive monocyte' rather than 'monocyte'; 'cutaneous melanoma' rather than 'melanoma'; 'aortic endothelial cell' rather than 'endothelial cell'). Generalize only when no specific term fits the evidence.

knockout_gene.prompt_description: Knockout gene refers to a gene that has been rendered completely non-functional or deleted from an organism's genome, often achieved by genome editing technologies like CRISPR/Cas9. e.g., BRCA1, TP53, EGFR, etc. When multiple ontology candidates match, prefer the MOST SPECIFIC descendant term over broader parent terms (e.g., 'CD14-positive monocyte' rather than 'monocyte'; 'cutaneous melanoma' rather than 'melanoma'; 'aortic endothelial cell' rather than 'endothelial cell'). Generalize only when no specific term fits the evidence.

knockdown_gene.prompt_description: Knockdown gene refers to a gene whose expression has been significantly reduced, but not entirely eliminated, typically realized by the introduction of siRNA or shRNA. e.g., BRCA1, TP53, EGFR, etc. When multiple ontology candidates match, prefer the MOST SPECIFIC descendant term over broader parent terms (e.g., 'CD14-positive monocyte' rather than 'monocyte'; 'cutaneous melanoma' rather than 'melanoma'; 'aortic endothelial cell' rather than 'endothelial cell'). Generalize only when no specific term fits the evidence.

overexpressed_gene.prompt_description: Overexpressed gene refers to a gene that is expressed at abnormally high levels compared to its normal physiological state, often achieved through plasmid transfection or viral transduction. e.g., BRCA1, TP53, EGFR, etc. When multiple ontology candidates match, prefer the MOST SPECIFIC descendant term over broader parent terms (e.g., 'CD14-positive monocyte' rather than 'monocyte'; 'cutaneous melanoma' rather than 'melanoma'; 'aortic endothelial cell' rather than 'endothelial cell'). Generalize only when no specific term fits the evidence.
